title: 50 Dollars
description: Right Excellent Errol Barrow, Barbados's first Prime Minister who led the nation to independence from Great Britain in 1966, appears on this orange polymer note. The reverse features Independence Square in Bridgetown with a statue honoring his legacy.
body: The Central Bank of Barbados issued the $50 note in December 2022, celebrating Errol Barrow (1920–1987), one of Barbados's most beloved national figures. Barrow led the Democratic Labour Party to victory in 1961 and became the nation's first Prime Minister upon independence in 1966, serving until 1976. He is credited as the architect of modern Barbados. The reverse showcases Independence Square in Bridgetown with Barrow's statue, symbolizing his enduring legacy. The note features the innovative De La Rue SPOTLIGHT™ holographic stripe, providing advanced security through interactive visual effects. The L-shaped tactile emboss aids visually impaired users. When all six denominations are arranged sequentially, they collectively form a map of Barbados.

title: 100 Dollars
description: Sir Grantley Herbert Adams, Barbados's first Premier and a central figure in the nation's social revolution, appears on this gray polymer note. The reverse features Grantley Adams International Airport in Christ Church, named in his honor.
body: The Central Bank of Barbados issued the $100 note in December 2022, honoring Sir Grantley Adams (1898–1971), Barbados's first Premier and one of the nation's 10 National Heroes. Adams was instrumental in the social revolution that transformed Barbados and guided the nation's path toward independence and prosperity. The reverse features Grantley Adams International Airport in Christ Church, the nation's primary gateway and a lasting monument to his leadership. The note includes the De La Rue SPOTLIGHT™ holographic stripe with sophisticated security elements. The hologram displays dolphins, broken tridents, Pride of Barbados flowers, and the numeral '100.' A square-shaped tactile emboss provides accessibility for visually impaired users. Watermarks featuring Adams's image add further security.

title: 10 Konvertibilnih Maraka
description: Mehmedalija 'Mak' Dizdar (1917–1971), an influential Bosnian poet, appears on this Federation variant note in Latin script. The reverse features a fragment of the Stećak Radimlja, a medieval tombstone representing Bosnia's rich cultural heritage.
body: The Central Bank of Bosnia and Herzegovina issued the 10 KM note on June 22, 1998, as the currency was established following the Dayton Agreement. The Federation variant (Latin script) features Mehmedalija 'Mak' Dizdar, one of Bosnia's most celebrated 20th-century poets. The note represents the dual-entity design system of BiH banknotes, with parallel Latin-script and Cyrillic-script variants that serve both the Federation of Bosnia and Herzegovina and Republika Srpska. The reverse displays a fragment of Stećak Radimlja, medieval tombstones that symbolize Bosnia's historical and cultural significance. The note includes advanced micro-optical security elements and iridescent features. Current notes from 2012 onward remain in full circulation; pre-2012 issues ceased to be legal tender on January 1, 2025.

title: 10 Konvertibilnih Maraka
description: Aleksa Šantić (1868–1924), a celebrated Serbian and Yugoslav poet, appears on this Republika Srpska variant note in Cyrillic script. The reverse features a loaf of bread, representing Šantić's literary legacy and cultural significance.
body: The Central Bank of Bosnia and Herzegovina issued the 10 KM Cyrillic-script note on June 22, 1998, representing Republika Srpska within the dual-entity banknote system. The note features Aleksa Šantić (1868–1924), a prominent Serbian and Yugoslav poet whose works are foundational to South Slavic literature. Unlike the Federation variant with its Stećak imagery, the Republika Srpska reverse displays a loaf of bread, symbolizing cultural and poetic themes in Šantić's work. Both variants—Latin-script (Federation) and Cyrillic-script (Republika Srpska)—are fully interchangeable legal tender throughout Bosnia and Herzegovina, reflecting the country's constitutional structure and post-war reconciliation through currency design. Current 2012+ issues remain in circulation; pre-2012 notes ceased legal tender on January 1, 2025.

title: 20 Konvertibilnih Maraka
description: Antun Branko Šimić (1898–1925), a Croatian expressionist poet, appears on this Federation variant note in Latin script. The reverse features a fragment of Stećak Radimlja, a medieval tombstone symbolizing Bosnia's cultural heritage.
body: The Central Bank of Bosnia and Herzegovina issued the 20 KM note on July 27, 1998, as part of the higher-denomination set following the initial currency launch. The Federation variant (Latin script) honors Antun Branko Šimić (1898–1925), a pioneering Croatian expressionist poet whose experimental work transformed Croatian literature. Šimić's portrait represents the Croatian cultural contribution within Bosnia and Herzegovina's multi-ethnic heritage. The reverse displays Stećak Radimlja, medieval tombstones that serve as powerful symbols of Bosnia's historical continuity and artistic legacy. Like all BiH banknotes, this note exists in both Latin (Federation) and Cyrillic (Republika Srpska) variants, both fully legal tender. Current 2012+ issues remain in circulation.

title: 20 Konvertibilnih Maraka
description: Filip Višnjić (1767–1847), a celebrated Serbian folk singer and poet, appears on this Republika Srpska variant note in Cyrillic script. The reverse features a different medieval tombstone design representing Bosnian cultural heritage.
body: The Central Bank of Bosnia and Herzegovina issued the 20 KM Cyrillic-script note on July 27, 1998, completing the higher-denomination set in the dual-entity banknote system. The note features Filip Višnjić (1767–1847), a legendary Serbian folk poet and singer whose epic poems are cornerstones of South Slavic oral tradition. Višnjić represents the Serbian cultural heritage within Bosnia and Herzegovina's constitutionally mandated balance between Federation and Republika Srpska. The reverse displays a different medieval stećak design from the Federation variant, reflecting the two entities' distinct cultural narratives while maintaining currency unity. Both 20 KM variants—Latin and Cyrillic—are fully interchangeable throughout the country. Enhanced security features distinguish 2012+ issues from older notes. Pre-2012 issues ceased legal tender on January 1, 2025.

title: 50 Konvertibilnih Maraka
description: This 50 KM note features Musa Ćazim Ćatić (Federation variant) or Jovan Dučić (Republika Srpska variant), representing Bosniak and Serbian literary heritage respectively. The reverse features Zgošća Stećak (Federation) or books and a pen symbolizing intellectual heritage (Republika Srpska).
body: The Central Bank of Bosnia and Herzegovina issued the 50 KM note on July 27, 1998, as the highest denomination in the initial banknote release. The Federation variant features Musa Ćazim Ćatić (1878–1915), a pivotal Bosniak Muslim poet and intellectual whose literary contributions shaped modern Bosnian culture. The Republika Srpska variant features Jovan Dučić (1871–1943), a Bosnian Serbian poet and diplomat whose diplomatic efforts advanced South Slavic literature and political thought. The 50 KM reverse in the Cyrillic variant displays books, reading glasses, and a pen—symbols of literary heritage. The Federation version features Zgošća Stećak, another medieval tombstone. Both variants remain fully legal tender. Enhanced 2012+ issues are in circulation; pre-2012 notes ceased legal tender January 1, 2025.

issuer: Central Bank of Bosnia and Herzegovina
title: 50 Konvertibilnih Maraka
description: Jovan Dučić (1871–1943), renowned Bosnian Serb poet and diplomat, appears on the obverse of this 50 KM note in the Republika Srpska variant (Cyrillic script). The reverse features literary symbols—books, reading glasses, and pen—honoring his profound contributions to Bosnian literature.
body: Originally issued on July 27, 1998, following the Dayton Agreement that ended the Bosnian War, this 50 KM note celebrates Jovan Dučić, one of Bosnia's most celebrated literary figures. The note exists in two interchangeable variants: a Federation version (Latin script) and a Republika Srpska version (Cyrillic script), reflecting Bosnia's unique political structure. The 2012 redesign introduced the Motion™ security thread, a 4mm windowed element featuring a quill that appears to move left and right when the note is tilted. Additional security measures include an optically variable ink on the denomination number (shifting from purple-red to greenish brown), watermark with Dučić's profile, and microprinting details.

issuer: Central Bank of Bosnia and Herzegovina
title: 100 Konvertibilnih Maraka
description: Nikola Šop (1904–1982), acclaimed Croatian-Bosnian poet, graces the obverse of this 100 KM note in the Federation variant (Latin script). The reverse features the Zgošća Stećak, a medieval monumental tombstone representing Bosnia's rich cultural heritage and ancient traditions.
body: First issued July 27, 1998, this 100 KM Federation note honors Nikola Šop, a prominent Yugoslav-era Bosnian Croat poet whose work shaped the region's literary landscape. The choice to feature the Zgošća Stećak on the reverse—an iconic medieval monumental stone—underscores Bosnia's continuous cultural identity spanning centuries. The 2012 series enhanced security with the Motion™ windowed security thread (4mm) featuring a quill that moves when tilted, creating an anti-counterfeiting visual effect. The denomination number employs optically variable ink that shifts between dark brown and greenish-gold hues depending on viewing angle. Watermark and microprinting provide additional authentication layers.

highlights.reason: Honors Nikola Šop, celebrated Croatian-Bosnian poet; celebrates medieval Bosnian heritage through Stećak imagery.
highlights.design: Obverse: Portrait of Nikola Šop, dark brown coloration on yellow underprint. Reverse: Zgošća Stećak medieval monumental tombstone.
highlights.security: Motion™ windowed security thread with moving quill, optically variable ink on denomination, watermark, microprinting

issuer: Central Bank of Bosnia and Herzegovina
title: 100 Konvertibilnih Maraka
description: Petar Kočić (1877–1916), distinguished Bosnian Serb writer, playwright, and political activist, appears on the obverse of this 100 KM note in the Republika Srpska variant (Cyrillic script). The reverse depicts literary symbols—books, pen, and reading glasses—reflecting his enduring impact on Bosnian Serb cultural and political life.
body: Issued July 27, 1998, this 100 KM Republika Srpska variant commemorates Petar Kočić, a pivotal figure in Bosnian Serb literature and nationalism who fought for Bosnian autonomy in the early 20th century. Kočić's life was cut short at age 39 during World War I, but his literary and political legacy profoundly influenced the region. The 2012 redesign introduced Motion™ security featuring a 4mm windowed security thread with a quill that appears to move when tilted. The denomination number shifts from dark brown to greenish-gold through optically variable ink technology. The note incorporates watermark imagery, microprinting, and enhanced materials to prevent counterfeiting.

highlights.reason: Honors Petar Kočić, Bosnian Serb writer and political activist; celebrates Bosnian literary and nationalist traditions.
highlights.design: Obverse: Portrait of Petar Kočić, dark brown coloration on yellow underprint, Cyrillic script. Reverse: Books, pen, and reading glasses symbolizing literary legacy.
highlights.security: Motion™ windowed security thread with moving quill motif, optically variable ink on denomination, watermark, microprinting

title: 20 Pula
description: Dr. Kgalemang Tumedisco Motsete (1899–1974), composer of Botswana's national anthem, appears on the red obverse of this 20 Pula note. The reverse showcases Botswana's mining heritage with the Orapa diamond mine installation and a stylized roan antelope, celebrating the nation's natural wealth.
body: First circulated on August 23, 2009, this 20 Pula note honors Dr. Kgalemang Tumedisco Motsete, whose composition of 'Fatshe Leno La Rona' (Our Land) became Botswana's national anthem. Measuring 138 × 69 mm, the note features Botswana's coat of arms with rearing zebras on the obverse. The reverse celebrates the Orapa diamond mine, one of Botswana's first major mining installations, alongside a roan antelope representing the nation's wildlife. Security includes a 12mm holographic stripe featuring the coat of arms and a color-switching kingfisher image, a StarChrome red-to-green windowed security thread with demetalized 'BOB 20' text, and raised embossing on the denomination for tactile identification. Two edge dots assist visually impaired users.

highlights.reason: Honors Dr. Kgalemang Tumedisco Motsete, composer of the national anthem; celebrates Botswana's mining sector.
highlights.design: Obverse: Portrait of Motsete in red coloration, Botswana coat of arms with zebras. Reverse: Orapa diamond mine conveyor systems, roan antelope.
highlights.security: 12mm holographic stripe with switching kingfisher image, StarChrome windowed security thread, watermark of rearing zebra, raised embossing on denomination, two edge dots for accessibility

title: 50 Pula
description: Sir Seretse Khama (1921–1980), founding President of Botswana, is prominently featured on the obverse of this brown and yellow 50 Pula note, accompanied by a malachite kingfisher. The reverse depicts the iconic Okavango Delta swamps with a traditional mokoro and African fish eagle, symbolizing Botswana's natural treasures.
body: Circulated since August 23, 2009, this 50 Pula note celebrates Sir Seretse Khama, Botswana's revered founding President who led the nation to independence in 1966 and established democratic governance. The obverse features Khama's portrait alongside the Botswana coat of arms and a malachite kingfisher, a symbol of the nation's natural heritage. The reverse showcases the Okavango Delta, one of Africa's most pristine wilderness areas, with a poler navigating a traditional mokoro (dugout canoe) beneath an African fish eagle—representing Botswana's commitment to wildlife conservation. Security features include a StarChrome windowed security thread with demetalized 'BOB 50' text and color-shifting properties, a watermark depicting a rearing zebra, and electrotype '50' details.

highlights.reason: Honors Sir Seretse Khama, founding President and architect of Botswana's independence and democracy.
highlights.design: Obverse: Portrait of Sir Seretse Khama in brown/yellow coloration, malachite kingfisher, Botswana coat of arms. Reverse: Okavango Delta scene with mokoro and African fish eagle.
highlights.security: StarChrome windowed security thread with demetalized BOB 50, watermark of rearing zebra, electrotype 50, cornerstones security element

title: 1 Brunei Dollar
description: His Majesty Sultan Hassanal Bolkiah Mu'izzaddin Waddaulah appears in formal military attire and traditional dastar on the obverse. The note features the bunga simpur (Dillenia suffruticosa), Brunei's national flower, alongside the Sultan Omar Ali Saifuddien mosque and the royal ceremonial barge (Mahligai).
body: Scheduled for circulation in the first half of 2026, this 1 Brunei Dollar note is part of the new GUARDIAN™ polymer series announced on September 22, 2025. The denomination presents Sultan Hassanal Bolkiah alongside the bunga simpur (Dillenia suffruticosa), Brunei's national flower. The obverse also features the Sultan Omar Ali Saifuddien mosque and the Mahligai (royal ceremonial barge), iconic symbols of Brunei's Islamic heritage. The GUARDIAN™ polymer substrate provides enhanced durability and waterproofing. DOMINO™ tactile marks—raised dots in a unique arrangement for this denomination—enable visually impaired users to identify the note.

highlights.reason: Introduces redesigned polymer currency series; honors Sultan Hassanal Bolkiah and celebrates Brunei's national botanical heritage.
highlights.design: Obverse: Portrait of Sultan Hassanal Bolkiah in military uniform and dastar, bunga simpur flower, Brunei coat of arms. Reverse: Sultan Omar Ali Saifuddien mosque, Mahligai ceremonial barge.
highlights.security: Color-shifting inks, dynamic window effects, DOMINO™ tactile marks (single dot pattern), GUARDIAN™ polymer substrate

title: 5 Brunei Dollars
description: Sultan Hassanal Bolkiah appears in military dress and dastar on the obverse of this 5 Brunei Dollar note alongside the bunga kunyit (Curcuma longa), the turmeric plant. The Lapau (Royal Ceremonial Hall) provides architectural context on the obverse design.
body: Part of the 2026 polymer banknote series announced in September 2025, the 5 Brunei Dollar note continues the tradition of honoring Sultan Hassanal Bolkiah while introducing a different native Brunei flower. The bunga kunyit (Curcuma longa), commonly known as turmeric, represents the nation's botanical diversity and its historical significance in traditional medicine and culinary practices throughout Southeast Asia. The Lapau, Brunei's Royal Ceremonial Hall, appears on the obverse as an important venue for state functions and cultural events. GUARDIAN™ polymer construction ensures durability in tropical climates. Color-shifting inks and dynamic window security elements provide authentication, while DOMINO™ tactile embossed marks in a multi-dot pattern unique to this denomination enable accessibility.

highlights.reason: Celebrates Brunei's botanical heritage through the turmeric plant; honors Sultan Hassanal Bolkiah with modernized polymer technology.
highlights.design: Obverse: Portrait of Sultan Hassanal Bolkiah in uniform and dastar, bunga kunyit (turmeric) flower, Lapau Royal Ceremonial Hall, Brunei coat of arms. Reverse: Complementary national iconography.
highlights.security: Optically variable inks, dynamic window effects, DOMINO™ tactile embossed marks (multi-dot pattern unique to denomination), GUARDIAN™ polymer substrate

title: 10 Brunei Dollars
description: Sultan Hassanal Bolkiah in formal military attire and traditional dastar is featured alongside the rancah rancah (Cosmos caudatus), an ornamental flowering plant. The impressive Jame' Asr Hassanil Bolkiah mosque in Bandar Seri Begawan dominates the reverse, showcasing Brunei's Islamic heritage.
body: Part of the 2026 GUARDIAN™ polymer series, the 10 Brunei Dollar note presents Sultan Hassanal Bolkiah alongside the rancah rancah (Cosmos caudatus), a flowering plant valued for both ornamental and culinary purposes. The Jame' Asr Hassanil Bolkiah mosque, one of Southeast Asia's largest and most spectacular mosque structures completed in 1994 and named after the Sultan, is featured on this denomination. GUARDIAN™ polymer construction ensures durability in Brunei's tropical climate. Color-shifting inks and dynamic window security elements provide authentication, while DOMINO™ tactile marks in a distinctive three-dot pattern arrangement enable visually impaired identification.

highlights.reason: Honors Sultan Hassanal Bolkiah and Brunei's Islamic heritage through the Jame' Asr Hassanil Bolkiah mosque; celebrates the rancah rancah ornamental flower.
highlights.design: Obverse: Sultan Hassanal Bolkiah in uniform and dastar, rancah rancah (Cosmos caudatus) flower, Brunei coat of arms. Reverse: Jame' Asr Hassanil Bolkiah mosque in Bandar Seri Begawan.
highlights.security: Color-shifting inks, dynamic window effects, DOMINO™ tactile marks (three-dot pattern), GUARDIAN™ polymer substrate

title: 100 Brunei Dollars
description: Sultan Hassanal Bolkiah appears in formal military dress and dastar on the obverse, accompanied by the kuduk-kuduk (Melastoma malabathricum), a striking blue-purple flowering plant native to Brunei. The Jame' Asr Hassanil Bolkiah mosque reinforces Islamic themes on the reverse.
body: As part of the 2026 GUARDIAN™ polymer series, the 100 Brunei Dollar note features Sultan Hassanal Bolkiah alongside the kuduk-kuduk (Melastoma malabathricum), known as Singapore rhododendron, with striking blue-purple blooms that hold medicinal significance in traditional Southeast Asian medicine. The Jame' Asr Hassanil Bolkiah mosque appears again on this denomination as a unifying design element, emphasizing Brunei's Islamic foundation. GUARDIAN™ polymer substrate provides exceptional durability for this high-circulation note, with advanced anti-counterfeiting measures including optically variable inks and sophisticated window features. DOMINO™ tactile embossed marks in a unique multi-dot arrangement specific to the 100 denomination assist visually impaired users.

highlights.reason: Celebrates Brunei's natural flora and Islamic heritage; advances security technology for higher denomination.
highlights.design: Obverse: Sultan Hassanal Bolkiah in military uniform and dastar, kuduk-kuduk (Melastoma malabathricum) flower, Brunei coat of arms. Reverse: Jame' Asr Hassanil Bolkiah mosque.
highlights.security: Optically variable inks, dynamic window effects with animated elements, DOMINO™ tactile marks (unique multi-dot pattern), GUARDIAN™ polymer substrate

title: 500 Brunei Dollars
description: Al-Marhum Sultan Haji Omar 'Ali Saifuddien Sa'adul Khairi Waddien, the former Sultan and father of the current reign, is honored on this 500 Brunei Dollar note. The obverse features the tepus kantan (Etlingera solaris), a medicinal flowering ginger plant representing Brunei's botanical heritage.
body: The highest denomination in the 2026 GUARDIAN™ polymer series, the 500 Brunei Dollar note pays tribute to Al-Marhum Sultan Haji Omar 'Ali Saifuddien Sa'adul Khairi Waddien (1914–1986), the previous Sultan and father of the current reigning Sultan. Omar 'Ali Saifuddien transformed Brunei from a protectorate into a sovereign nation and established the foundation of modern Brunei Darussalam. The note features the tepus kantan (Etlingera solaris), a flowering ginger plant valued in traditional Brunei medicine and cuisine. This denomination employs the most advanced security features in the series. GUARDIAN™ polymer construction combined with state-of-the-art anti-counterfeiting measures ensures the 500 note resists fraud and counterfeiting.

highlights.reason: Honors former Sultan Omar 'Ali Saifuddien who established modern Brunei as a sovereign nation; celebrates medicinal ginger plant heritage.
highlights.design: Obverse: Portrait of Al-Marhum Sultan Haji Omar 'Ali Saifuddien, tepus kantan (Etlingera solaris) flowering ginger, Brunei coat of arms. Reverse: National imagery honoring Brunei's historical leadership.
highlights.security: Advanced color-shifting inks, sophisticated window features with animated effects, DOMINO™ tactile marks (most distinctive pattern for highest denomination), GUARDIAN™ polymer substrate

title: 20 Yuan
description: A golden dragon in flight dominates the front of this commemorative note, celebrating the Year of the Dragon 2024. The reverse features children performing a traditional dragon dance with lanterns.
body: Issued to commemorate the 2024 Lunar New Year and the Year of the Dragon, this polymer banknote was released on February 10, 2024, with approximately 100 million notes entering circulation. The obverse displays a dragon alongside the national emblem of the People's Republic of China, while the reverse depicts children performing a dragon dance with traditional lanterns in red and gold tones. The note features the denomination '20' in green SICPA SPARK ORIGIN® security ink and includes text in Mongolian, Tibetan, Uyghur, and Zhuang scripts. This is the first in a planned 12-year commemorative series celebrating each Chinese zodiac animal.

highlights.reason: To commemorate the 2024 Lunar New Year and the Year of the Dragon in Chinese zodiac tradition
highlights.design: Obverse: Dragon in flight with national emblem of China. Reverse: Children performing traditional dragon dance with red lanterns.
highlights.security: Green SICPA SPARK ORIGIN® denomination marking, polymer substrate, multilingual text (Mongolian, Tibetan, Uyghur, Zhuang)

title: 1 Dollar
description: The obverse features a portrait of Queen Elizabeth II alongside the coat of arms of the Cayman Islands, with angel fish and a map. The reverse depicts the Bluff on Cayman Brac and a nautilus shell.
body: Issued by the Cayman Islands Monetary Authority, the 1 Dollar note serves as the primary currency for everyday transactions throughout the Cayman Islands. The design incorporates Queen Elizabeth II as the primary portrait alongside national symbols of the island. Security features include an advanced windowed security thread, watermark of a turtle, and anti-counterfeiting measures such as microprinting, color-shifting denomination indicators, and texture elements for tactile verification by the visually impaired.

highlights.reason: Standard circulating currency for the Cayman Islands
highlights.design: Obverse: Queen Elizabeth II portrait, Cayman Islands coat of arms, angel fish, map. Reverse: Bluff on Cayman Brac, nautilus shell.
highlights.security: Windowed security thread with demetallized elements, watermark with turtle and CIMA electrotype, hidden Queen portrait visible under light, reflective security strip with micro-images, color-shifting denomination, tactile text elements

title: 25 Dollars
description: The 25 Dollar note features Queen Elizabeth II on the obverse alongside the Cayman Islands coat of arms. The banknote is part of the current circulating series issued by the Cayman Islands Monetary Authority.
body: The 25 Dollar banknote was issued in August 1999 and remains part of the current circulating currency of the Cayman Islands. All notes have been printed by De La Rue currency note printers in England and feature advanced security measures. The banknote series includes six denominations ($1, $5, $10, $25, $50, and $100) with Queen Elizabeth II portrayed on the obverse of all notes. Each denomination incorporates a security thread, watermark, and serial numbers to prevent counterfeiting.

highlights.reason: Standard circulating currency for higher-value transactions in the Cayman Islands
highlights.design: Obverse: Queen Elizabeth II portrait with Cayman Islands national symbols and coat of arms. Reverse: Local landmark with national symbols.
highlights.security: Security thread, watermark, serial numbers, De La Rue printing technology, enhanced security features

title: 100 Pesos
description: The obverse features Carlos Manuel de Céspedes (1819–1874), a leading figure in Cuba's independence movement and first president of the Ten Years' War. The reverse depicts the Anti-Imperialist Tribune 'José Martí' in Havana.
body: Issued by the Banco Central de Cuba, the 100 Pesos note honors Carlos Manuel de Céspedes, who led Cuba's first independence war against Spanish colonialism. The 2024 edition includes updated security features with microprinted security threads bearing the text 'PATRIA O MUERTE VENCEREMOS', personalized watermarks with denomination numbers, and tactile relief for identification by the visually impaired. The note incorporates the Braille system alongside Arabic numerals and letters for accessibility. The reverse showcases the monument to the Anti-Imperialist Tribune José Martí in central Havana.

highlights.reason: To honor Carlos Manuel de Céspedes, leader of Cuba's Ten Years' War for independence
highlights.design: Obverse: Carlos Manuel de Céspedes portrait with national symbols. Reverse: Anti-Imperialist Tribune 'José Martí' monument in Havana.
highlights.security: Microprinted security thread with 'PATRIA O MUERTE VENCEREMOS', personalized watermark with denomination, Braille system indicators, tactile relief elements, UV-visible security markings

title: 500 Pesos
description: The obverse features Ignacio Agramonte y Loynáz (1841–1873), a prominent leader in Cuba's independence struggle. The reverse depicts the Constituent Assembly at Guáimaro held on April 10, 1869.
body: The 500 Pesos note, released in 2024, celebrates Ignacio Agramonte, a key military commander during Cuba's Ten Years' War and author of the constitution adopted at Guáimaro. The banknote is printed on cotton fiber paper in a predominant green color. The 2024 issue includes notes printed by both Impresos de Seguridad in Havana and Goznak in Russia, differentiated by their series markings (IG and IGA respectively). The segmented security thread is microprinted with 'BCC 500' and is readable from both sides, while three green intaglio dots near the upper right corner enable identification by the visually impaired.

highlights.reason: To honor Ignacio Agramonte, military leader and constitutional author of Cuba's independence movement
highlights.design: Obverse: Ignacio Agramonte y Loynáz portrait. Reverse: Constituent Assembly at Guáimaro (April 10, 1869), depicting the founding of the first Cuban republic.
highlights.security: Segmented microprinted security thread with 'BCC 500' readable from both sides, three green intaglio dots for blind identification, personalized watermarks, Braille system indicators

title: 50 Kroner
description: The 50 Kroner note features the Sallingsund Bridge on the front with Danish bridges as the design theme. The reverse displays the Skarpsalling Pot, representing Danish prehistoric artifacts.
body: Issued by Danmarks Nationalbank, the 50 Kroner banknote is part of the 'Bridges of Denmark' series introduced in 2009. An upgraded 2009A version is entering circulation from February 2026 with enhanced security features while maintaining the original Sallingsund Bridge design on the obverse and Skarpsalling Pot on the reverse. The series interprets the theme of Danish bridges as connections between various regions and as links between past and present, as conceptualized by artist Karin Birgitte Lund. These upgraded notes precede the planned entirely new banknote series expected in 2028-2029.

highlights.reason: Upgraded security version of the 'Bridges of Denmark' series, representing Denmark's infrastructure and cultural heritage
highlights.design: Obverse: Sallingsund Bridge spanning Sallingsund strait. Reverse: Skarpsalling Pot, a significant Danish Bronze Age artifact.
highlights.security: Raised tactile copper print for 'Danmarks Nationalbank' and signatures, windowed Motion security thread with dynamic movement when tilted, half numerals appearing complete when held to light, enhanced anti-counterfeiting measures

issuer: Banco Central de la República Dominicana
title: 50 Pesos Dominicanos
description: The 50 Pesos note features the Cathedral of Santa María la Menor in Santo Domingo, the first cathedral constructed in the Americas. The reverse displays Dominican national heritage symbols.
body: The 50 Pesos Dominicanos note honors the Catedral Primada de América (Cathedral of Santa María la Menor) in Santo Domingo, the first cathedral built in the Americas, constructed between 1510 and 1540. The 2024 version continues the design tradition of celebrating Dominican architectural and cultural landmarks. The banknote incorporates multiple security features including watermarks, security threads, variable magnetic ink, and microprints to prevent counterfeiting.

highlights.reason: To commemorate the Cathedral of Santa María la Menor, the first cathedral built in the Americas
highlights.design: Obverse: Cathedral of Santa María la Menor (Catedral Primada de América) in Santo Domingo. Reverse: Dominican national symbols and heritage elements.
highlights.security: Watermarks, security thread, variable magnetic ink, microprints, hologram elements, color security features

issuer: Banco Central de la República Dominicana
title: 100 Pesos Dominicanos
description: The 100 Pesos note features three Dominican founding fathers: Francisco del Rosario Sánchez, Juan Pablo Duarte, and Matías Ramón Mella, who led the Dominican independence movement of 1844.
body: The 100 Pesos Dominicanos banknote honors the three founding fathers of the Dominican Republic: Francisco del Rosario Sánchez, Juan Pablo Duarte, and Matías Ramón Mella. These three revolutionary leaders founded the secret society 'La Trinitaria' in 1838, which culminated in Dominican independence from Haiti on February 27, 1844. The 2024 version celebrates these national heroes with their triple portrait displayed prominently on the obverse. The banknote incorporates modern security measures including watermarks, security threads, microprints, and color-shifting elements.

highlights.reason: To honor the three founding fathers of Dominican independence: Sánchez, Duarte, and Mella
highlights.design: Obverse: Triple portrait of Francisco del Rosario Sánchez, Juan Pablo Duarte, and Matías Ramón Mella. Reverse: Dominican national symbols and independence movement imagery.
highlights.security: Watermarks, security thread, variable magnetic ink, microprints, hologram elements, color-shifting security features

issuer: Banco Central de la República Dominicana
title: 200 Pesos Dominicanos
description: The 200 Pesos note features the three Mirabal sisters—Patria, Minerva, and María Teresa—who were prominent anti-Trujillo activists murdered for their political opposition.
body: The 200 Pesos Dominicanos banknote honors the Mirabal sisters: Patria, Minerva, and María Teresa Mirabal, who became national symbols of resistance against Dominican dictator Rafael Leónidas Trujillo. These three sisters founded the resistance movement 'Las Mariposas' (The Butterflies) and were brutally murdered on November 25, 1960. Their deaths sparked widespread international condemnation and contributed to Trujillo's eventual downfall. The 2024 banknote celebrates their legacy with their portraits prominently displayed on the obverse. The note incorporates modern security features including watermarks, security threads, microprints, and holograms.

highlights.reason: To commemorate the Mirabal sisters (Patria, Minerva, María Teresa), martyrs of the anti-Trujillo resistance
highlights.design: Obverse: Triple portrait of the Mirabal sisters. Reverse: Dominican national symbols and commemoration of the resistance movement.
highlights.security: Watermarks, security thread, variable magnetic ink, microprints, hologram elements, color-shifting features, tactile security elements

issuer: Banco Central de la República Dominicana
title: 500 Pesos Dominicanos
description: The 500 Pesos note features Salomé Ureña de Henríquez, a pioneering Dominican poet and educator, and her son Pedro Henríquez Ureña, a renowned philosopher and literary scholar.
body: The 500 Pesos Dominicanos banknote honors Salomé Ureña de Henríquez (1850–1897), revered as one of the most distinguished Dominican poets and founders of the Instituto de Señoritas, and her son Pedro Henríquez Ureña (1884–1946), a towering figure in literature and philosophy across Latin America. The 2024 version continues to celebrate their legacy with their dual portraits prominently displayed on the obverse. The reverse displays the headquarters of the Central Bank of the Dominican Republic. The banknote incorporates modern security measures including watermarks, security threads, microprints, holograms, and variable magnetic ink.

highlights.reason: To honor Salomé Ureña de Henríquez and Pedro Henríquez Ureña, pioneers of Dominican literature, education, and philosophy
highlights.design: Obverse: Dual portrait of Salomé Ureña de Henríquez and Pedro Henríquez Ureña. Reverse: Central Bank of the Dominican Republic headquarters.
highlights.security: Watermarks, security thread, variable magnetic ink, microprints, hologram elements, color-shifting features, advanced printing technology

title: 50 Dollars
description: A polymer commemorative note featuring Eric Gairy, Grenada's first Prime Minister, and Olympic sprinter Kirani James. The obverse depicts a family silhouette, the map of Grenada, a nutmeg branch, and a jet airliner at Maurice Bishop International Airport. The reverse shows Maurice Bishop (second Prime Minister), the city of St. George's, and cultural scenes with a woman singing and men drumming.
body: Issued in August 2024 to commemorate Grenada's 50th anniversary of independence (1974–2024), this polymer banknote represents a historic moment for the Eastern Caribbean Currency Union. The note was released in limited circulation initially through commercial banks and credit unions in Grenada, Carriacou, and Petite Martinique, and became legal tender throughout the ECCU member countries. The design celebrates Grenada's cultural heritage, featuring national heroes, natural resources like the nutmeg crop that is central to the island's economy, and symbols of modern infrastructure.

highlights.reason: Commemorate Grenada's 50th anniversary of independence (1974–2024)
highlights.design: Obverse: Eric Gairy (first PM), Kirani James (Olympic sprinter), family silhouette, Grenada map, nutmeg branch, Maurice Bishop International Airport jet. Reverse: Maurice Bishop (second PM), St. George's, nutmeg branch, woman singing with three men drumming.
highlights.security: SPOTLIGHT™, PUREIMAGE™, ROTATE™, Enhanced GEMINI™ security features providing level 1 and level 2 security

title: 100 Lari
description: A violet and red banknote honoring Shota Rustaveli, the great medieval Georgian poet (1160–1220). The obverse features his portrait with illustrations from his epic poem 'The Knight in the Tiger Skin,' along with a VII-century bas-relief of 'Daniel in the Den of Lions' from Martvili Cathedral.
body: First introduced in February 2016 and upgraded in 2020 with enhanced security features, this 100 lari note celebrates Georgia's literary heritage through Shota Rustaveli, whose 12th-century epic poem 'The Knight in the Tiger Skin' is considered the cornerstone of Georgian national identity. The 2020 upgrade significantly enhanced security printing to prevent counterfeiting while maintaining the cultural significance of the design. The note's color scheme—violet and red—reflects traditional Georgian artistic aesthetics, and the inclusion of the national opera house on the reverse emphasizes Georgia's contribution to world culture and arts.

highlights.reason: Honor Shota Rustaveli, medieval Georgian poet and literary icon, as part of the National Bank's series celebrating Georgian cultural heritage
highlights.design: Obverse: Shota Rustaveli portrait, 'The Knight in the Tiger Skin' illustrations and manuscript pages, VII-century 'Daniel in the Den of Lions' bas-relief. Reverse: Zakaria Paliashvili National Academic Theater of Opera and Ballet, Georgian national anthem fragment.
highlights.security: Holographic stripe with Rustaveli portrait and flowers, blue-to-red windowed security thread with demetalized text, watermark with Rustaveli portrait, iridescent stripe, latent image, optical variable ink, microlettering, UV fluorescent properties

title: 1 Quetzal
description: A commemorative polymer note featuring the resplendent quetzal bird and General José María Orellana, who established the quetzal as Guatemala's currency in 1924. The obverse displays Mayan iconography including Temple I from Tikal and Mayan number glyphs.
body: Reintroduced in September 2024, this commemorative 1 quetzal polymer banknote marks the 100th anniversary of Guatemala's national currency (1924–2024). The quetzal bird, Guatemala's national symbol, represents freedom and cultural pride, while General José María Orellana's portrait honors the statesman who modernized Guatemala's financial system. The integration of Mayan heritage through Temple I and glyphs reflects Guatemala's deep connection to its pre-Columbian past. The Varifeye ColourChange holographic patch provides advanced authentication.

highlights.reason: Commemorate the 100th anniversary of the quetzal currency (1924–2024) and honor General José María Orellana who established it
highlights.design: Obverse: Resplendent quetzal bird, Mayan glyph registration device, José María Orellana portrait, Temple I (Tikal), Guatemalan flag. Reverse: Banco de Guatemala headquarters building, Mayan glyphs, Guatemalan flag.
highlights.security: Varifeye ColourChange gold-to-blue holographic patch, windowed security thread, polymer substrate, Mayan glyph and flag registration devices

issuer: Banque Centrale de la République de Guinée
title: 1.000 Francs Guinéens
description: A tan-colored note featuring a woman with traditional plaits hairstyle, representing Guinea's diverse ethnic heritage. The reverse displays an open-pit bauxite mining scene depicting Guinea's important mineral resource industry.
body: Part of Guinea's ongoing currency series featuring women and national symbols, the 1,000 francs note celebrates the nation's economic foundation in mining and the role of women in Guinean society. First issued in 2015 and continuing through 2024, this denomination remains a standard circulation note. The design reflects Guinea's identity as one of the world's leading bauxite producers, with the mining imagery on the reverse highlighting the industry's importance to the national economy.

highlights.reason: Standard circulation note featuring women and national symbols; celebrates Guinea's mining heritage
highlights.design: Obverse: Woman with traditional plaits, national coat of arms, flying pigeons. Reverse: Open-pit bauxite mining scene with dump trucks and steam shovel.
highlights.security: Windowed security thread with demetalized GNF 1000, bank logo registration device, watermark featuring woman with plaits and electrotype 1000

issuer: Banque Centrale de la République de Guinée
title: 5.000 Francs Guinéens
description: A purple, green, red, and blue note featuring a woman wearing a traditional cimier headwrap hairstyle. The reverse depicts Guinea's map, the Kinkon Dam representing hydroelectric development, and a corn cob representing agricultural heritage.
body: Introduced in 2015 as part of Guinea's modern banknote series, the 5,000 francs note continues through 2024, combining cultural representation with economic symbolism. The woman with cimier hairstyle represents Guinea's diverse ethnic traditions and the important role of women in the nation's social fabric. The Kinkon Dam featured on the reverse exemplifies Guinea's infrastructure development and progress in renewable energy generation. The agricultural imagery through the corn motif underscores the importance of agriculture alongside mining in Guinea's economy.

highlights.reason: Standard circulation note celebrating women, national infrastructure, and economic development
highlights.design: Obverse: Woman with cimier hairstyle, pigeons in flight, national coat of arms. Reverse: Map of Guinea, Kinkon Dam (hydroelectric facility), corn cob.
highlights.security: 3mm red-to-green windowed security thread with demetalized GNF 5000, bank logo registration device, watermark with woman wearing cimier hairstyle and electrotype 5000

issuer: Banque Centrale de la République de Guinée
title: 20.000 Francs Guinéens
description: A blue, green, orange, and purple note featuring a Guinean woman wearing a traditional head scarf. The reverse shows Guinea's map, the Kaleta hydroelectric dam, and electricity transmission towers symbolizing modern infrastructure.
body: First issued in 2018 and continuing through 2024, the 20,000 francs note represents Guinea's highest denomination, celebrating both cultural heritage and modern development. The woman in traditional head scarf imagery affirms women's central role in Guinean society and economy. The Kaleta Dam represents Guinea's strategic investment in hydroelectric power to support industrial and economic growth. This higher denomination reflects Guinea's commitment to facilitating larger transactions while maintaining strong security features and cultural representation.

highlights.design: Obverse: Woman wearing traditional head scarf, pigeons in flight, national coat of arms. Reverse: Map of Guinea, Kaleta hydroelectric dam, electricity transmission towers.
highlights.security: 3mm red-to-green windowed security thread with demetalized GNF 20000, bank logo registration device, watermark with Guinean woman and electrotype 20000

issuer: Banque Centrale de la République de Guinée
title: 2.000 Francs Guinéens
description: A green-colored note featuring a man wearing a traditional cap, representing male participation in Guinea's cultural and economic life. The reverse depicts Guinea's map, palm trees, and Mount Nimba—the nation's highest mountain and a UNESCO World Heritage site.
body: Introduced in 2018 as part of Guinea's expanded denomination series, the 2,000 francs note continues through 2024, bridging the gap between smaller and larger denominations. The man in traditional cap represents Guinea's diverse male population and cultural heritage. Mount Nimba featured on the reverse holds significant importance as the highest point in Guinea and a protected natural area with unique biodiversity. The palm tree imagery emphasizes Guinea's tropical environment and agricultural sector.

issuer: Banque Centrale de la République de Guinée
title: 500 Francs Guinéens
description: The lowest denomination in Guinea's modern circulation series, featuring a portrait and national iconography on the obverse. Revised in 2019 with updated security features to prevent counterfeiting.
body: First circulated in the modern series and revised in 2019, the 500 francs note serves as Guinea's lowest denomination in active circulation, facilitating small daily transactions for the general population. The 2019 revision strengthened security features to combat counterfeiting while maintaining the design aesthetic. This denomination reflects the Central Bank's commitment to maintaining a complete range of notes suited to the Guinean economy's transaction needs, from the smallest everyday purchases to major commercial operations.

title: 500 Dollars
description: A lilac-brown and purple note featuring Guyana's national coat of arms and a red parrot on the obverse. The reverse depicts the Parliament Building in Georgetown, palm trees, and a cannon. In 2024 the note received a Rolling Star® Lead Pure security foil featuring a Macaw that shifts from magenta to green when tilted.
body: First issued in 2011 as part of the modern Guyanese currency series, the 500 dollar note celebrates Guyana's natural wealth and democratic heritage. The parrot imagery reflects the country's diverse wildlife and rainforest ecosystem, while the Parliament Building represents the foundation of Guyanese governance. The 2024 upgrade replaced the holographic stripe with the advanced Rolling Star® Lead Pure security foil, incorporating a colorful Macaw that demonstrates dynamic security properties when the note is tilted.

highlights.reason: Standard circulation note celebrating Guyana's biodiversity and governance; 2024 upgrade added Rolling Star® security foil
highlights.design: Obverse: Bank of Guyana logo, national coat of arms, red parrot, Guyanese map. Reverse: Parliament Building in Georgetown, palm trees, cannon.
highlights.security: Rolling Star® Lead Pure security foil with Macaw (magenta-to-green shift), holographic elements with bank logo, windowed security thread, watermark with parrot and electrotype 500

title: 1.000 Dollars
description: A brown and orange note featuring the Bank of Guyana logo and national coat of arms alongside a red macaw parrot and the Victoria Regia lily (Victoria amazonica). The reverse displays the Bank of Guyana building in Georgetown. In 2024 the note received RAPID® Vision technology featuring a Jaguar with pulse effects.
body: First issued in 2019 as the highest denomination in regular circulation, the 1,000 dollar note showcases Guyana's national symbols and institutional pride. The Victoria Regia lily, the world's largest water lily native to the Amazon and found in Guyanese waters, represents the nation's natural heritage. The macaw represents the rich avian diversity of Guyana's rainforests. The 2024 security upgrade replaced RAPID® micro-optic with RAPID® Vision, a 6mm brown/green feature displaying a Jaguar with dynamic pulse effects, significantly enhancing counterfeiting prevention.

highlights.reason: Highest denomination in regular circulation; 2024 upgrade added RAPID® Vision Jaguar security feature
highlights.design: Obverse: Bank of Guyana logo, national coat of arms, red macaw parrot, Victoria Regia lily, Guyanese map. Reverse: Bank of Guyana building in Georgetown.
highlights.security: RAPID® Vision security feature with Jaguar (6mm brown/green with pulse effect), holographic elements with bank logo and macaw, blue windowed security thread with demetalized 1000, watermark with macaw parrot head and Victoria Regia lily

title: 50.000 Rupiah
description: Dr. H. Djuanda Kartawidjaja, Indonesia's 10th Prime Minister (1911–1963), featured on the obverse alongside the national coat of arms. Reverse depicts the Tari Legong traditional dance and Komodo National Park.
body: Issued as part of Bank Indonesia's continued circulation of modern rupiah denominations honoring national figures. Djuanda Kartawidjaja is recognized for the historic Djuanda Declaration of 1957, establishing Indonesia's maritime boundaries. The 50,000 rupiah note measures 146 mm × 65 mm with a predominantly blue color scheme. The reverse features Komodo National Park, reflecting Indonesia's rich biodiversity, alongside the Legong dance representing cultural heritage. The note incorporates a windowed security thread with printed BI denomination marking.

highlights.reason: To honor Djuanda Kartawidjaja, a key historical figure in Indonesian political independence and maritime sovereignty

title: 1.000 Yen
description: Shibasaburo Kitasato (1852–1931), pioneering bacteriologist who co-discovered the tetanus and plague bacilli, featured on the obverse. Reverse depicts The Great Wave off Kanagawa, the iconic ukiyo-e print by Katsushika Hokusai.
body: Issued July 3, 2024, marking the Bank of Japan's first comprehensive banknote redesign in 20 years. Shibasaburo Kitasato revolutionized modern medicine through his pioneering work in bacteriology, co-discovering the tetanus bacillus and plague bacillus. The reverse features Hokusai's masterpiece, celebrating both scientific achievement and artistic heritage. The 1,000 yen note incorporates revolutionary 3D holographic portraiture—the world's first banknote application of this technology—where the hologram rotates when tilted. Additional security includes high-definition watermarks, latent images, intaglio printing for accessibility, and microprinting.

highlights.reason: First major redesign of Japanese banknotes in 20 years; honors Shibasaburo Kitasato with world's first 3D holographic portrait on a circulating banknote
highlights.design: Obverse: Shibasaburo Kitasato portrait. Reverse: The Great Wave off Kanagawa by Hokusai, symbolizing Japanese artistic tradition.
highlights.security: 3D holographic portrait (world first for banknotes, rotates when tilted), watermarks, latent images, intaglio printing, microprinting

title: 5.000 Yen
description: Umeko Tsuda (1864–1929), pioneering educator and founder of Tsuda University, featured on the obverse. Reverse depicts wisteria flowers, reflecting traditional Japanese botanical aesthetics.
body: Issued July 3, 2024, as part of the Bank of Japan's first redesign in 20 years. Umeko Tsuda was a pioneering advocate for women's education in Japan, establishing Tsuda University as a center of women's higher learning. The reverse wisteria flowers represent both natural beauty and cultural significance in Japanese art and literature. The note features the same revolutionary 3D holographic portraiture as the other new denominations, with dynamic tilting effects creating patterns and numerals that shift when viewed from different angles. Additional security includes watermarks, latent images, and intaglio textures.

highlights.design: Obverse: Umeko Tsuda portrait. Reverse: Wisteria flowers representing traditional Japanese botanical and artistic heritage.
highlights.security: 3D holographic portrait with dynamic effects, watermarks, latent images, intaglio printing, microprinting

title: 10.000 Yen
description: Eiichi Shibusawa (1840–1931), the 'Father of Japanese Capitalism' and pioneering industrialist, featured on the obverse. Reverse depicts Tokyo Station's Marunouchi Building, an iconic symbol of modern Japanese architecture and economic development.
body: Issued July 3, 2024, honoring Eiichi Shibusawa's transformative role in Japan's economic modernization. Shibusawa founded numerous enterprises and financial institutions, fundamentally shaping modern Japanese capitalism. The reverse features Tokyo Station's Marunouchi Building, completed in 1914, symbolizing Japan's architectural and economic progress. The 10,000 yen note includes innovative 3D holographic portraits that rotate with tilting motion. Additional security measures include watermarks, latent images, intaglio printing patterns for tactile accessibility, and microprinting.

title: 1.000 Tenge
description: Features the 'Tree of Life' with bird motif from the Issyk Kurgan burial mound (5th–4th centuries BCE). Reverse depicts Kazakh dog breeds—the Tazy sighthound and Tobet (Central Asian Shepherd Dog).
body: Issued March 28, 2024, as part of the National Bank of Kazakhstan's new 'Saka Style' series celebrating ancient Eurasian heritage. The obverse features the 'Tree of Life' with soaring bird and DNA spiral with infinity symbol, bridging ancient Saka civilization with contemporary science. The reverse showcases the Tobet and Tazy dog breeds against a stylized shield ornament—these dogs were essential companions of nomadic peoples for hunting and livestock protection. The series represents a pioneering blend of ancient cultural symbols with modern design elements and was awarded Best New Banknote Series at the international High Security Printing Conference.

highlights.reason: To celebrate Kazakhstan's heritage from the ancient Saka civilization and honor the 'Saka Style' as the ancestor of traditional Kazakh ornament
highlights.design: Obverse: Tree of Life from Issyk Kurgan with soaring bird, DNA spiral and infinity symbol. Reverse: Tobet and Tazy dog breeds with stylized shield ornament.
highlights.security: Advanced security printing techniques; award-winning design from High Security Printing Conference 2024

title: 2.000 Tenge
description: Features the 'Tree of Life' with bird motif from the Issyk Kurgan burial mound. Reverse depicts a herd of Saiga antelope (Saiga tatarica) against a landscape of stylized hills and a lake forming an infinity ornament.
body: Issued December 25, 2024, continuing the National Bank of Kazakhstan's 'Saka Style' series. The 2,000 tenge note features harmonious green and blue tones, reflecting the steppe landscape. The obverse displays the Tree of Life symbol with DNA spiral, emphasizing the connection between ancient heritage and modern scientific understanding. The reverse showcases four standing Saiga antelope—an endangered Central Asian steppe species—against a landscape forming an infinity ornament, representing the boundless steppe ecosystem and the continuity of nature.

description: Features the 'Tree of Life' with bird motif from the Issyk Kurgan burial mound (5th–4th centuries BCE). Reverse depicts a snow leopard (Panthera uncia) set against snow-covered mountain peaks of Kazakhstan's Tian Shan range.

The 10,000 tenge note is colored in shades of violet, blue, and white, reflecting the alpine environment. The obverse displays the ancient Tree of Life symbol alongside DNA spiral and infinity ornament, bridging ancient Saka civilization with contemporary science. The reverse features the snow leopard ornament—an endangered species symbolic of Kazakhstan's commitment to wildlife conservation—with snow-covered Tian Shan mountains in the background.

title: 50 Shillings
description: Features the Kenyatta International Convention Centre (KICC) on the obverse, Kenya's iconic architectural symbol in Nairobi. Reverse depicts renewable energy installations, emphasizing Kenya's commitment to green energy and sustainable development.
body: Originally issued May 31, 2019, and reissued in 2024 with updated security features and new signatures from Central Bank Governor Kamau Thugge. Mandated by Kenya's 2010 Constitution, the 2019 series removed portraits of known individuals in favor of national monuments and thematic designs. The reverse depicts green energy installations including wind turbines, geothermal, and solar power, reflecting Kenya's renewable energy initiatives. The 2024 updated version includes an enhanced security thread and the Crystal effect security feature for added anti-counterfeiting protection.

highlights.security: Crystal effect security feature, enhanced security thread, updated governor signature

description: Features the Kenyatta International Convention Centre (KICC) on the obverse. Reverse depicts social services, recognizing Kenya's healthcare, education systems, and athletic achievements.

In compliance with Kenya's 2010 Constitution, the note excludes portraits of individuals and instead celebrates the nation's social institutions. The reverse depicts social services, specifically recognizing Kenya's contributions in healthcare, education, and athletics. The 2024 updated version incorporates the ZigZag dynamic movement security effect, which creates movement when tilted, alongside updated signatures from Central Bank Governor Kamau Thugge.

title: 500 Shillings
description: Kenyatta International Convention Centre with Nairobi skyline on the obverse. Reverse features Kenya's national parks, sandy beaches, and lion imagery representing tourism.
body: The 500 Shillings note was issued in August 2024 by the Central Bank of Kenya as part of a series recognizing four major updates including new signatures and enhanced security features. The obverse features the iconic Kenyatta International Convention Centre monument alongside the Nairobi skyline, Kenya's national coat of arms, and a shield with dove symbolizing peace. The reverse showcases Kenya's tourism heritage with depictions of national parks, wildlife, and sandy beaches featuring the simba (lion). The note incorporates advanced RollingStar i+ security threads with brilliant color-shifting properties and the distinctive ZigZag dynamic movement effect on the embedded security thread, creating an illusion of fluid motion when tilted. Printed by Giesecke & Devrient (G&D), the 2024 series won the Best New Series of Banknotes award at the HSP-EMEA Conference in Basel, Switzerland in February 2025.

title: 1.000 Shillings
description: Kenyatta International Convention Centre with Nairobi skyline on the obverse. Reverse features an aerial view of Kenya's Parliament building representing governance and legislative authority.
body: The 1,000 Shillings note was issued in August 2024 as the flagship denomination of the Central Bank of Kenya's upgraded banknote series. The obverse displays the Kenyatta International Convention Centre with Nairobi skyline, the national coat of arms, a peace dove, and one of Kenya's Big Five animals. The reverse features a sophisticated aerial perspective of Kenya's Parliament building, symbolizing national governance and the sovereignty of the people. The brown banknote with maroon and multicolored underprint incorporates cutting-edge security features including the RollingStar i+ thread with magenta-to-green color-shifting properties and the ZigZag dynamic movement effect that creates a mesmerizing visual security element when the note is tilted. Produced by Giesecke & Devrient (G&D), the 2024 series received international recognition for its innovative security features, winning the Best New Series of Banknotes award in February 2025.

title: 10 Maloti
description: Portrait of King Moshoeshoe I on obverse. Reverse features cosmos flowers on an African landscape representing Lesotho's natural heritage.
body: The 10 Maloti banknote was officially released by the Central Bank of Lesotho on December 23, 2021, as part of a comprehensive redesigned series featuring enhanced security and reduced dimensions to accommodate advanced protective features. The obverse displays a portrait of one of Lesotho's historic kings alongside the national coat of arms and the Mokorotlo (Basotho hat) symbol, with intaglio printing creating tactile surfaces. The reverse showcases cosmos flowers on an African landscape in red and light rose tones, celebrating Lesotho's natural beauty. The note incorporates multiple security features including an Anima security thread that appears broken but becomes continuous when held to light, a watermark of King Moshoeshoe I wearing a top hat, and tactile parallel lines for visually impaired identification. Produced by De La Rue using ADDvance hybrid substrate combining cotton and wood pulp with sustainable certification, the series represents an upgrade from the 2010 series while maintaining design continuity.

title: 20 Maloti
description: Portrait of King Letsie III on obverse. Reverse features traditional Basotho round houses (rondavels) with thatched roofs in a landscape setting.
body: The 20 Maloti banknote entered circulation on December 23, 2021, as part of Lesotho's comprehensive redesigned currency series featuring King Letsie III on the obverse alongside the national coat of arms and the distinctive Basotho hat registration device. The reverse depicts traditional Lesotho architecture—rondavels with thatched roofs—set in a landscape that reflects the country's cultural heritage and pastoral traditions. Measuring 135 mm × 70 mm, the note incorporates an Anima security thread that appears broken during normal viewing but becomes continuous when held to light, demonstrating advanced security printing technology. Additional protective features include a watermark of King Moshoeshoe I, tactile parallel lines for accessibility to the visually impaired, and the Mokorotlo (Basotho hat) see-through registration device. The 2021 series was produced by De La Rue using sustainable ADDvance hybrid materials and represents the first banknote series using Cotton Made in Africa certification, combining 75% cotton with 25% FSC-certified wood pulp.

highlights.reason: 2021 redesign to modernize Lesotho's currency with enhanced security, reduced size, and sustainable materials reflecting national identity

title: 50 Maloti
description: Portrait of King Moshoeshoe I on obverse. Reverse features men on horseback in traditional Basotho dress riding across a mountain background.
body: The 50 Maloti banknote was released on December 23, 2021, featuring King Moshoeshoe I on the obverse along with the national coat of arms and Mokorotlo symbol. The reverse depicts traditional Basotho horse riders in indigenous dress riding across a mountainous landscape, celebrating the cultural significance of horsemanship in Lesotho's heritage. Measuring 138 mm x 70 mm, the note incorporates the RollingStar dynamic security thread that displays striking color-shifting and light effects, replacing the simpler Anima thread used in lower denominations. This advanced security feature shows brilliant visual dynamics between CBL letters and denomination markings when tilted or moved. Additional security elements include a watermark of King Moshoeshoe I wearing a top hat, tactile elements for the visually impaired, the Mokorotlo registration device, and anti-bacterial SICPA Protect varnish coating. Produced by De La Rue using sustainable ADDvance hybrid substrate, the 50 Maloti represents a significant step up in security within the 2021 series.

The reverse depicts a traditional Lesotho shepherd tending his flock of sheep on a mountain landscape with stylized palm tree design, celebrating shepherding as integral to Basotho cultural and economic traditions. The note incorporates the RollingStar dynamic security thread featuring advanced color-shifting properties and brilliant light effects responding to viewer angle and movement. title: 200 Maloti
description: Portrait of King Moshoeshoe I on obverse. Reverse features a traditional Lesotho merchant of handicrafts mounted on horseback carrying merchandise.
body: The 200 Maloti banknote was officially released on December 23, 2021, as the highest denomination in Lesotho's redesigned currency series, featuring King Moshoeshoe I on the obverse alongside the national coat of arms and Mokorotlo symbol. The reverse depicts a traditional Lesotho merchant of handicrafts mounted on horseback, carrying merchandise across the mountainous landscape. Measuring 160 mm x 69 mm, the highest dimension in the series, the note showcases the most advanced security features including the RollingStar dynamic security thread with color-shifting properties creating brilliant light effects when tilted. Uniquely, the 200 Maloti was manufactured using sustainable materials comprising 75% Cotton Made in Africa and 25% FSC-certified wood pulp through the ADDvance hybrid substrate, representing Lesotho's commitment to environmentally responsible currency production.

title: 20 Dollars
description: President William V.S. Tubman, longest-serving Liberian president, on obverse. Reverse features an outdoor market scene representing commerce and everyday life.
body: The $20 Liberian banknote was officially issued on October 6, 2022, honoring President William V.S. Tubman, the 18th President of Liberia and the nation's longest-serving head of state. The obverse prominently features a portrait of President Tubman, symbolizing his significant contributions to Liberian history and governance. The reverse depicts a vibrant outdoor market scene with men and women engaged in commerce, the Central Bank of Liberia seal, and traditional economic imagery reflecting Liberia's mercantile heritage. The note measures 133 x 66 mm and features a sophisticated color scheme combining brown, tan, and purple tones. Manufactured with advanced security features including a gold-to-green windowed security thread with demetalized stars that display pulsing and dynamic color-changing effects when viewed at different angles, the $20 denomination provides strong counterfeit protection. The note also incorporates a see-through registration device with Liberian star fragments and clear visibility of CBL and denomination when held to light.

highlights.security: Gold-to-green windowed security thread with demetalized stars showing pulsing and dynamic color-changing effects, see-through registration device with Liberian star fragments, CBL and denomination visible under light

title: 50 Dollars
description: President Samuel K. Doe, the 20th President of Liberia, on obverse. Reverse features a man trimming an oil palm tree representing Liberia's agricultural heritage.
body: The $50 Liberian banknote was officially released on October 6, 2022, honoring President Samuel K. Doe, the 20th President of Liberia. The obverse prominently features a portrait of President Doe alongside imagery of palm plantations. The reverse depicts a worker trimming an oil palm tree, the Central Bank of Liberia seal with crossed Kissi money symbols, and front and back imagery of a 1969 50-cent coin, creating a historical connection between Liberia's monetary past and present. The note measures 133 x 66 mm and features an elegant color scheme combining red, brown, and purple tones. The banknote incorporates a purple-to-gold windowed security thread with demetalized stars displaying pulsing and dynamic color-changing effects that shift brilliantly as the note is rotated. Additional security features include a see-through registration device with Liberian star fragments and clear visibility of CBL text and denomination markings when held to light.

title: 500 Dollars
description: Seven women designers of the Liberian flag, led by Susanna Lewis, on obverse. Reverse features a hippopotamus family and the Capitol Building in Monrovia.
body: The $500 Liberian banknote was officially issued on October 6, 2022, as a commemorative tribute to the seven women who designed the Liberian flag. The obverse features portraits of these pioneering women designers, led by Susanna Lewis who served as chairperson of the design committee, alongside Matilda Newport, Rachel Johnson, Mary Hunter, Mrs. J.B. Russwurm, Colonette Teage Ellis, and Sarah Draper. This historically significant design honors the often-overlooked female contributors to Liberia's national symbols and heritage. The reverse depicts an adult hippopotamus with her calf set against the backdrop of the Capitol Building in Monrovia, along with the Central Bank of Liberia seal. The note measures 133 x 66 mm and employs sophisticated purple, blue, and yellow color tones. The $500 denomination features a green-to-blue windowed security thread with demetalized stars displaying pulsing and dynamic color-changing effects when rotated at different angles, plus a see-through registration device with Liberian star fragments.

highlights.reason: Released 2022 to honor the seven women who designed the Liberian flag and celebrate Liberia's cultural heritage
highlights.design: Obverse: Portraits of seven flag designers including Susanna Lewis, Matilda Newport, Rachel Johnson, Mary Hunter, J.B. Russwurm, Colonette Teage Ellis, Sarah Draper. Reverse: Adult hippopotamus with calf, Capitol Building in Monrovia, Central Bank seal
highlights.security: Green-to-blue windowed security thread with demetalized stars showing pulsing and dynamic color-changing effects, see-through registration device with Liberian star fragments, CBL and denomination visible under light

title: 20 Ringgit
description: Hawksbill and Leatherback sea turtles in Malaysian waters, representing the marine conservation theme.
body: The 20 Ringgit note from the fourth series of Malaysian banknotes features two sea turtle species endemic to Malaysian waters: the Hawksbill Turtle (Eretmochelys imbricata) and the Leatherback Turtle (Dermochelys coriacea). The obverse displays the first Yang di-Pertuan Agong (King) of Malaysia, maintaining the royal theme across the series. Reintroduced in the fourth series after being absent from the third series, the note represents Malaysia's commitment to marine conservation and biodiversity. The fourth series was released in July 2012 under the theme 'Distinctively Malaysia,' featuring traditional expressions, natural wonders, flora, fauna, and cultural heritage.

highlights.reason: Reintroduction of denomination to fourth series (2012) representing Malaysia's marine biodiversity and conservation efforts
highlights.design: Obverse: First Yang di-Pertuan Agong with Hawksbill and Leatherback sea turtles. Reverse: Marine life and Malaysian waters.

title: 100 Ringgit
description: Two UNESCO World Heritage Sites: Mount Kinabalu in Sabah and the limestone formations of Mulu National Park in Sarawak.
body: The 100 Ringgit note showcases Malaysia's two most prominent natural wonders, both declared UNESCO World Heritage Sites. The obverse features the first Yang di-Pertuan Agong, while Mount Kinabalu in Kinabalu Park, Sabah (Southeast Asia's highest mountain) and the spectacular limestone pinnacle rock formations of Gunung Api Valley in Mulu National Park, Sarawak are prominently displayed. The fourth series was released in July 2012 as part of the 'Distinctively Malaysia' theme, celebrating the nation's biodiversity, cultural heritage, and natural beauty.

highlights.reason: Part of fourth series (2012) celebrating Malaysia's UNESCO World Heritage natural wonders
highlights.design: Obverse: First Yang di-Pertuan Agong. Reverse: Mount Kinabalu and Mulu limestone pinnacles with landscape imagery.
highlights.security: Colour-shifting security thread with Bunga Raya image, gold-coloured stripe with RM100 text, pixel watermark portrait with highlighted numerals, micro lens thread, clear window

title: 25 Rupees
description: Sir Moilin Jean Ah-Chuen, prominent Sino-Mauritian politician and businessman, with national coat of arms.
body: The 25 Rupee note features Sir Jean Etienne Moilin Ah-Chuen (1911-1991), a significant Sino-Mauritian politician and businessman who served as Minister from 1967 to 1976 and contributed greatly to Mauritius's economic and industrial development after independence. The obverse includes the national coat of arms, Justice with sword and scales, and the Bank of Mauritius building facade. The reverse displays a building facade, a man with a trap, and a map of Rodrigues Island, reflecting the nation's cultural heritage.

highlights.reason: Part of the 1999 series honoring significant historical figures in Mauritian independence and economic development
highlights.design: Obverse: Sir Moilin Jean Ah-Chuen with coat of arms and Justice symbol. Reverse: Building facade, cultural figure, and Rodrigues Island map.
highlights.security: Spider conch shell registration device, windowed security thread with demetalized BANK OF MAURITIUS, watermark of dodo bird head

title: 100 Rupees
description: Renganaden Seeneevassen, early Mauritian politician, with Court House architecture and dodo bird imagery.
body: The 100 Rupee note honors Renganaden Seeneevassen (1910-1958), a prominent Mauritian politician who served as president from 1956 to 1958. The obverse features his portrait alongside the coat of arms, Justice with sword and scales, and the Bank of Mauritius building facade. The note includes inscriptions in English, Hindi, and Tamil, reflecting Mauritius's linguistic diversity. The reverse displays the Court House building and trees. An upgraded polymer version was introduced in 2025 with enhanced security elements including a dodo in a transparent window.

highlights.reason: Part of the 1999-2024 series recognizing Renganaden Seeneevassen's contribution to early Mauritian political development
highlights.design: Obverse: Renganaden Seeneevassen with coat of arms and Justice. Reverse: Court House building and landscape elements.
highlights.security: Spider conch shell registration device, windowed security thread with demetalized BANK OF MAURITIUS, watermark of dodo bird head, blue-to-gold Swing dodo egg

title: 200 Rupees
description: Sir Abdool Razack Mohamed, pre- and post-independence minister, with Port Louis Central Market imagery.
body: The 200 Rupee note features Sir Abdool Razack Mohamed (1906-1978), an Indian-born senior minister who served in both pre- and post-independence cabinets of Mauritius, playing a crucial role in the nation's political development. The obverse displays his portrait alongside the Bank of Mauritius building facade, Justice with sword and scales, and a red-to-green Swing dodo egg security feature. Inscriptions appear in English, Hindi, and Tamil, celebrating Mauritian cultural diversity. The reverse depicts produce stalls at the Port Louis Central Market, reflecting the nation's economic heritage.

highlights.reason: Part of the 1999-2024 series honoring Sir Abdool Razack Mohamed's contribution to Mauritian governance and independence
highlights.design: Obverse: Sir Abdool Razack Mohamed with coat of arms and Justice. Reverse: Port Louis Central Market produce stalls.

title: 1.000 Rupees
description: Sir Charles Gaetan Duval, barrister and political leader, with Chateau de Reduit presidential residence.
body: The 1,000 Rupee note honors Sir Charles Gaetan Duval QC (1930-1996), a distinguished Mauritian barrister, statesman, and politician who led the Parti Mauricien Social Democrate (PMSD). The obverse features his portrait with the coat of arms, Justice with sword and scales, and holographic security elements. The reverse depicts the Chateau de Reduit (the official residence of Mauritius's President), emphasizing the nation's political heritage. A polymer version released in 2024 includes the Dodo bird within a transparent window and SPARK Flow PRIME security featuring gold-to-green color-shifting.

highlights.reason: Part of the 1999-2024 series recognizing Sir Charles Gaetan Duval's leadership and legal contributions to independent Mauritius
highlights.design: Obverse: Sir Charles Gaetan Duval with coat of arms and Justice. Reverse: Chateau de Reduit presidential residence.
highlights.security: Spider conch shell registration device, windowed security thread with demetalized BANK OF MAURITIUS, holographic patch with dodo, watermark of dodo bird head, SPARK Flow PRIME color-shifting (polymer version)

title: 2.000 Rupees
description: Sir Seewoosagur Ramgoolam, first Prime Minister of Mauritius, with ox cart and sugarcane agricultural imagery.
body: The 2,000 Rupee note features Sir Seewoosagur Ramgoolam (1900-1985), a distinguished Mauritian politician, statesman, and philanthropist who served as the island's first Chief Minister, Prime Minister, and Governor-General, earning recognition as the father of Mauritian independence. The obverse displays his portrait at left with the Mauritius coat of arms and blindfolded Lady Justice holding balance scales and sword. The reverse depicts a man with an ox cart carrying sugarcane, reflecting Mauritius's agricultural heritage and sugar industry that formed the backbone of the colonial economy. A polymer version released in 2018 features a transparent window with a dodo and Swing security feature visible from both sides.

highlights.reason: Honors Sir Seewoosagur Ramgoolam as first Prime Minister and architect of Mauritian independence
highlights.design: Obverse: Sir Seewoosagur Ramgoolam with coat of arms and Justice. Reverse: Ox cart with sugarcane in agricultural landscape.
highlights.security: Windowed security thread with demetalized BANK OF MAURITIUS, watermark of dodo bird head, transparent window with dodo and Swing feature (polymer version)

title: 50 Tögrög
description: Damdin Sukhbaatar with Soyombo national symbol; reverse features mountains, fir trees, and grazing horses.
body: The 50 Togrog note from Mongolia's 2019-2024 series features Damdin Sukhbaatar, a key figure in Mongolian independence, wearing a traditional peaked hat. The obverse includes the Soyombo national symbol and the denomination in gold SPARK Orbital security element. The reverse displays fir trees, mountains, and two horses grazing in a Mongolian landscape, celebrating the nation's natural heritage. Printed on brown paper and measuring 135 x 64 mm, the 2024 issue continues the 2019 series with enhanced security features including a 3D macro optic strip with motion surface, color-changing SPARK feature, and tactile lines for visually impaired people.

highlights.reason: Standard series banknote (2019-2024) honoring Damdin Sukhbaatar, hero of Mongolian independence
highlights.design: Obverse: Damdin Sukhbaatar with Soyombo symbol and SPARK Orbital denomination. Reverse: Mountains, fir trees, and grazing horses.
